text: Robotic instruments are used to lift the stomach, revealing the omentum, a layer of fat that needs to be divided. The surgeon aims to create a tube from the stomach while preserving the right gastroepiploic vessel visible on the left of the screen. The splenic artery is seen at the bottom of the screen, with tissue being divided above it. The diaphragm, a muscular canopy, is visible, and the esophagus is separated from the cruce of the diaphragm at the esophageal hiatus.

The surgeon lifts the stomach and continues to divide the omentum, carefully preserving the right gastroepiploic artery and vein, which run along the bottom portion of the stomach. The hook cautery device is used to separate layers of fat, enhancing the stomach's mobility for its eventual repositioning into the chest. The purple structure visible within the fat is identified as the right gastroepiploic vein.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide tissue while sealing blood vessels to prevent bleeding. The liver and gallbladder are visible, with the gallbladder appearing as a purple bag-like structure. The duodenum, the first portion of the small intestine, is exposed within the fat being divided. Tension is applied to the duodenum to facilitate tissue division, aiding in the mobilization of the stomach for its movement into the chest. The portohepatous, where the common bile duct is located, is lifted along with the gallbladder. The duodenum is positioned behind the right colon and mesentery, which are separated to free the duodenum.

The surgeon visually identifies the colon on the left side of the screen and the duodenum at the top right corner. The pancreas, appearing as yellowish peach-colored tissue, is attached to the duodenum. The surgeon continues to separate the right colon from the duodenum, a process known as cocorizing, to mobilize the duodenum for repositioning into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the duodenum by rolling it off the retroperitoneum, revealing the large purplish vena cava vein. The tissue over the vena cava is divided to allow the duodenum to be moved into the upper abdomen, facilitating the stomach's reach into the chest. A pyloromyotomy is performed using electrocautery to divide the muscle fibers of the pylorus, preventing contraction and ensuring food can exit the stomach smoothly. The surgeon then performs a lymphadenectomy, removing lymph nodes draining the esophagus and stomach, using hook cautery to dissect tissue around hepatic artery lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies the coronary vein, also known as the left gastric vein, and proceeds to dissect around it, preparing to divide it at its base. The hepatic artery is exposed, and lymph nodes are carefully lifted off the artery to ensure complete removal of nodal tissue draining the esophagus and upper stomach. The coronary vein is traced as it crosses behind the hepatic artery and inserts into the portal vein, with nodal tissue being removed for pathological examination.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the coronary vein, revealing the left gastric artery behind it. The vessel sealer is then used to seal the left gastric artery. Following this, the surgeon continues the lymphadenectomy, removing lymph node tissue from the area of the splenic artery up towards the cruce of the diaphragm, which is visible as a muscular structure.

The surgeon frees the remaining connective tissue around the esophagus, exposing the diaphragm. A hernia tape is wrapped around the esophagus and tied with a square knot, creating a handle for potential manipulation of the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to create a gastric conduit by forming a tube from the stomach. The stapler, six centimeters in length, is fired multiple times to construct the tube, which will serve as the new esophagus. The gastric blood vessel along the bottom edge of the stomach is preserved to ensure adequate blood supply to the conduit.

The surgeon completes the creation of the gastric conduit and begins the mediastinal dissection. Using umbilical tape to apply tension on the esophagus, the surgeon dissects around the native esophagus visible at the top of the screen. The procedure progresses from the abdomen into the chest cavity, known as the mediastinum, which is flanked by the lungs and contains the esophagus, heart, and aorta. A lymph node is identified and removed to check for cancer. The esophagus is lifted off the aorta, fully mobilized, and divided, creating a free specimen that is sent for pathological examination.

The surgeon pulls the gastric conduit, or new esophagus, up into the chest and secures it with sutures to a drain to maintain orientation. The specimen is placed in a retrieval pouch for pathology. The patient is repositioned on their left side, exposing the cut edge of the esophagus with the staple line and drain. The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus towards the upper chest, dissecting around the azagus vein, which crosses over the esophagus, and divides it with a stapler to create a window for further dissection.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the esophagus, ensuring it is free to allow for a long anastomosis. The stomach conduit is pulled up into the chest, reaching the top portion of the lung. A hole is made in both the stomach conduit and the esophagus, and a stapler is used to create a large opening between them. A nasogastric tube is inserted through the nose into the stomach to aid healing and prevent leaks. The common opening left by the stapler is closed with sutures in two layers using robotic instruments.

The surgeon completes the first layer of the esophagogastric anastomosis by tying down the sutures, then begins the second layer using interrupted sutures to further secure the connection between the esophagus and stomach. The completed anastomosis is irrigated, and the chest cavity is cleaned, ensuring the drain is correctly positioned. This marks the completion of a minimally invasive transthoracic esophagogastrectomy.
